did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a multistage fully connected residual neural network (deep learning) for the automatic classification of skin melanoma and other skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset and ISIC-2018 challenge data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a 'multistage fully connected residual neural network' for skin lesion classification. It reports specific results (96.07% accuracy) on the HAM10000 dataset and ISIC-2018 challenge data, compares performance against state-of-the-art methods, and details the methodology, including architecture design and training parameters, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Lesion Analysis Toward Accurate Detection of Melanoma using Multistage Fully Connected Residual Network
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automatic skin lesion classification, specifically for melanoma detection, by proposing a multistage fully connected residual neural network trained end-to-end. The authors utilized the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 training images across seven diagnostic classes, to address class imbalance through sampling of minority classes. The proposed method was evaluated against state-of-the-art techniques and the top performers of the ISIC-2018 challenge. Experimental results demonstrated that the model achieved a classification accuracy of 96.07% and an AUC of 0.886. These findings indicate that the proposed approach significantly outperforms previous ISIC-2018 competition winners and other existing methods in detecting skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for training and evaluation. Original count: 327 images; upsampled to 6705.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs automatic classification of skin lesions using a multistage fully connected residual neural network. The task is explicitly defined as classifying dermoscopic images into seven diagnostic categories from the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete class labels, with performance measured by accuracy (96.07%), AUC (0.886), sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for training and evaluation. It contains images across seven diagnostic classes.

use.training: Used for training the multistage fully connected residual neural network. The minority classes were upsampled to 6705 images each to handle class imbalance, resulting in a total of 46,935 images for training.
use.other: Used for evaluation of the proposed method's performance (96.07% accuracy) and comparison with state-of-the-art methods and ISIC-2018 challenge winners.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: Data from the ISIC-2018 skin lesion analysis challenge, which includes the HAM10000 training set and separate validation/test sets provided by the challenge organizers.
classes_used: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions

use.training: The training portion (HAM10000) was used for training.
use.validation: The validation set (193 images) was part of the challenge data structure, though the paper primarily reports results on the training set after upsampling.
use.testing: The testing set (1512 images) was part of the challenge data structure.
use.other: Used for benchmarking and comparison against the top performers of the ISIC-2018 challenge.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, etc.) against state-of-the-art methods and ISIC-2018 challenge winners without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed FcResNet-TL achieved a classification accuracy of 96.07% and an AUC of 0.886 on the HAM10000 dataset. This outperformed the winner of the ISIC-2018 challenge.

ISIC-2018 Challenge Comparison.notes: The proposed FcResNet-TL showed significant gain (0.891) in primary matrix value compared to the first five position holders of the ISIC-2018 challenge. It also demonstrated better performance in sensitivity, precision, and F-score compared to the challenge winner.

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained from scratch (weights initialized as in He et al. [26]; no ImageNet pretraining reported for the proposed model)
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (dataset images were upsampled to balance classes, but specific input dimensions like 224x224 are not stated in the provided text)

architecture_details.type: Multistage fully connected residual neural network
architecture_details.structure: Consists of multiple dense stages, where each stage is a mini dense residual network. Each stage contains a series of mini residual blocks. Each mini residual block consists of a series of 1x1, 3x3, 1x1 convolution layers followed by batch normalization and ReLU. Shortcut connections skip one or more layers to avoid vanishing gradients. Each layer receives feature maps from all preceding layers in the block.

stages.stage_1: Series of 3 mini residual blocks. Convolution filters sizes: 1x1, 3x3, 1x1 with kernel sizes 64, 64, 256 respectively.
stages.stage_2: Series of 4 mini residual blocks. Convolution filters sizes: 1x1, 3x3, 1x1 with kernel sizes 128, 128, 512 respectively.
stages.stage_3: Series of 6 mini residual blocks. Kernel sizes constantly increasing in each stage.

architecture_details.transition_layer: Included in FcResNet-TL variant. Consists of a 1x1 convolutional layer and a 2x2 average pooling layer at the end of each stage.
architecture_details.connectivity: Fully connected between blocks within stages (e.g., Res1 connected to Res2 and Res3 in stage 1).
architecture_details.final_layers: Average pooling layer with filter width of 1, followed by a fully connected layer, and a softmax layer.

training_details.optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (implied by momentum usage)
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but inverse frequency weighting was used to determine the weight of the loss function to address class imbalance.
training_details.training_schedule: Trained from scratch. Stopped when there was no improvement in the error rate of the validation set for 30 epochs.
training_details.data_augmentation: Not explicitly detailed for the proposed model in the text (mentioned for other works like Kassani et al. and Hagerty et al.). The paper notes upsampling minority classes to 6705 images each to handle imbalance.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer followed by Softmax
classification_or_output_head.classes: 7 diagnostic categories (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Vascular lesion)

preprocessing_techniques: Upsampling of minority classes to balance class distribution (replicating samples to equal majority class count of 6705)
Data augmentation including random cropping, random vertical and horizontal flipping, rotation (up to 90 degrees), shear (up to 20 degrees), and color adjustments (contrast, saturation, and hue)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Minority classes were upsampled by replicating samples to equal the majority class count (Melanocytic nevus) of 6705 images each, resulting in a total of 46,935 training images.
Applied as part of the data augmentation pipeline.
Applied as part of the data augmentation pipeline.
Adjustments to contrast, saturation, and hue were applied.

conclusions: The proposed multistage fully connected residual neural network (FcResNet-TL) achieved a classification accuracy of 96.07% and an AUC of 0.886 on the HAM10000 dataset, demonstrating significant improvement over state-of-the-art methods.

The proposed FcResNet-TL model outperformed the winner of the ISIC-2018 challenge, achieving a higher primary matrix value (0.891 vs 0.885) and superior performance in sensitivity (0.964 vs 0.833), precision (0.960 vs 0.826), and F-score (0.961 vs 0.823).
The study addresses the challenge of class imbalance in skin lesion analysis by upsampling minority classes to match the majority class count, which helped mitigate bias in the prediction model.

The fully automatic classification method leverages a three-stage deep residual neural network trained end-to-end without relying on prior knowledge of the data, effectively handling complex skin image features such as hair, skin, and nails.

The proposed deep learning approach showed significantly better performance compared to the top five position holders of the ISIC-2018 competition, indicating its potential for accurate early detection of melanoma.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by upsampling minority classes to match the majority class count (6705 images), resulting in a total of 46,935 training images; this synthetic balancing may introduce bias or overfitting to replicated samples.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance comparisons against state-of-the-art methods and ISIC-2018 challenge winners.
The model was trained from scratch without reported ImageNet pretraining, which may impact generalization compared to models using transfer learning.

The dataset contains significant class imbalance in its original form (e.g., Dermatofibroma: 115 images vs. Melanocytic nevus: 6705 images), which the authors mitigated via upsampling rather than alternative imbalance handling techniques.

The paper compares performance against the top five position holders of the ISIC-2018 challenge but does not provide detailed information on the specific architectures or hyperparameters of the competing models beyond their reported metrics.

The study focuses on automatic classification of seven skin lesion types, with specific emphasis on melanoma detection, but does not address external validation on independent datasets outside of the ISIC challenge structure.
The proposed FcResNet-TL achieved 96.07% accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming the ISIC-2018 winner (95.8% accuracy), but the evaluation is limited to the specific challenge data split.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset (part of the ISIC-2018 challenge) for training and evaluation. The original dataset contained 10,015 images with significant class imbalance. To address this, the authors upsampled minority classes to match the majority class count (6,705 images), resulting in a balanced training set of 46,935 images. The model was trained from scratch on this upsampled data. The paper reports results on the training set (after upsampling) and compares performance against the top performers of the ISIC-2018 challenge. The paper does not explicitly report a held-out internal test set split for the proposed model's final evaluation metrics, nor does it perform cross-dataset validation on a separate external cohort. The evaluation is primarily based on the ISIC-2018 challenge data structure.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper does not report training on one dataset and testing on a different, unseen named dataset. The evaluation is confined to the ISIC-2018 challenge data (HAM10000). While the ISIC-2018 challenge provided separate validation (193 images) and test (1,512 images) sets, the paper primarily focuses on the training set (upsampled) for model development and reports results that appear to be on the training data or the challenge's official test set without explicitly detailing a separate internal hold-out test split for the proposed method's primary metrics. There is no evidence of training on one source and testing on another distinct external dataset.

training.counts: 10,015 original images; 46,935 images after upsampling minority classes to 6,705 each
training.notes: Used for training the FcResNet and FcResNet-TL models from scratch. Minority classes were upsampled to handle class imbalance.

validation.notes: Mentioned as part of the ISIC-2018 challenge structure. The paper states training stopped when there was no improvement in the error rate of the validation set for 30 epochs, implying its use for early stopping/model selection, though specific validation metrics for the proposed model are not detailed in the results tables.

testing.notes: Part of the ISIC-2018 challenge structure. The paper compares the proposed model's performance against the top 5 winners of the ISIC-2018 challenge, which were evaluated on this test set. The reported metrics (e.g., 96.07% accuracy) are likely derived from the challenge's official test set or the training set (the text is slightly ambiguous, but the comparison with challenge winners implies test set evaluation). However, the paper explicitly states '96.07% accuracy' in the context of the HAM10000 dataset results table, which often refers to the training set in this specific paper's context (Table III lists results on 'HAM10000 Dataset using Up sampled dataset'). The comparison with ISIC-2018 winners (Table V) uses metrics like Primary Matrix Value, AUC, etc., which are challenge-specific.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The paper states 'train the network from scratch' and does not report using ImageNet or other pre-trained weights for the final FcResNet models. It mentions other works used ImageNet, but not this study's primary models.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Minority classes were upsampled by replicating samples to equal the majority class count (6,705 images). Data augmentation included random cropping, flipping, rotation, shear, and color adjustments.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: The ISIC-2018 challenge provided 10,015 training images, 193 validation images, and 1,512 test images. The authors used the training set (upsampled) for model fitting. The validation set was used for early stopping. The test set was used for final comparison with challenge winners.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: Not reported. The study did not use k-fold cross-validation.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: The ISIC-2018 test set (1,512 images) served as the unseen data for comparison with challenge winners. No other external datasets were used.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results reported in Table III on the upsampled HAM10000 dataset. Note: The paper is ambiguous whether these metrics are on the training set or the challenge test set. Given the table title 'Results of different architectures on HAM10000 Dataset using Up sampled dataset', it likely refers to the training set performance or a re-evaluation on the training set. However, the comparison with ISIC-2018 winners (Table V) suggests the model was also evaluated on the official test set.

Results from Table V, comparing against ISIC-2018 challenge winners. The accuracy of 0.961 is very close to the 0.9607 reported for the upsampled training set, raising questions about whether the same data was used or if the test set performance is being reported. The paper claims 'super-passing' the challenge winners.

within_dataset_results.split_or_cv: Upsampled training set (46,935 images) and ISIC-2018 validation (193 images) and test (1,512 images) sets

generalizability_notes: The study relies solely on the HAM10000 dataset from the ISIC-2018 challenge. There is no external validation on independent datasets from different institutions or populations. The use of upsampling to balance classes may introduce bias or overfitting to replicated samples. The paper does not report patient-level separation, relying on image-level splits, which risks data leakage if multiple images from the same patient are in different splits (though ISIC challenge splits are typically designed to mitigate this, the paper does not explicitly confirm patient-level separation for its own experiments).
The model was trained from scratch without ImageNet pretraining, which may limit generalizability compared to transfer learning approaches.
information_not_reported: Explicit confirmation of patient-level separation in the ISIC-2018 splits for the proposed model's evaluation.
Detailed breakdown of metrics per class for the proposed model on the test set.

Whether the 96.07% accuracy reported in Table III is on the training set or the test set (ambiguity exists, but context suggests training set or re-evaluation on training set, while Table V suggests test set evaluation with similar metrics).
Specific hyperparameters for the FcResNet-TL model beyond learning rate and momentum.
